description: secretx-env/src/lib.rs:52-53 std::env::var returns a String that is a freshly allocated UTF-8 copy of the underlying libc 'environ' entry. After SecretValue::new wraps the bytes in Zeroizing, only that copy is zeroed on drop — the original NUL-terminated string in environ remains in plain heap memory for the lifetime of the process and may be visible in /proc/self/environ, core dumps, and in copies made by every fork or exec call. This is a fundamental property of env-var-sourced secrets and cannot be fixed in this crate, but the lib.rs and README docs do not mention it. Users may reasonably assume secretx universally zeroizes secrets and not realise that env-sourced values leak the originating copy. Fix: add a # Security section to lib.rs and README documenting (a) the environ residue, (b) the implication for /proc/self/environ and core dumps, (c) the recommendation to prefer file or HSM-backed backends for high-sensitivity secrets.
title: Secret value is not fully zeroizable through env-var path
